tldr: Headlines almost universally frame Iran's nuclear program as an active weapons-proximity threat, with US strikes on nuclear sites and 'frighteningly close' language dominating; Trump-Xi summit alignment against a nuclear Iran provides the one visible diplomatic anchor, while deal collapse is the political throughline.

evidence.quote: Iran 'frighteningly close' to developing nuclear weapons
US and Israeli strikes hit Iran sites tied to nuclear weapon work
Possible new fortifications near Natanz, think tank says
any Iran deal must close plutonium pathway to nuclear bombs
U.S., China agreed Iran can never have nuclear weapon
China, US agree that Iran can never have nuclear weapon
Chinese, Iranian diplomats meet before US-Iran nuclear talk
Trump Calls Iran's Latest Proposal 'Piece Of Garbage'
Stopping Iran's nuclear program more important than Americans' economic pain
Trump faces split among retired US commanders
Bigger Geopolitical Take Outs from Iran War
Would We Be Better Off Today With the JCPOA?
How a post-Cold War uranium program proved even old foes can work together

isolation_top.note: Foreign Policy's JCPOA counterfactual headline is structurally a question, not a news assertion — analytical register semantically distant from breaking-news corpus
Unique satellite-imagery and Natanz fortification angle; specificity of 'tunnel complex' and 'tank' vocabulary absent from other titles
Live-blog headline format ('Xi-Trump summit live') structurally different from article titles; real-time update register inflates semantic distance
Unique 'frighteningly close' proximity framing via US Energy Secretary — emotional-intensity register distinct from procedural diplomatic corpus
Kyiv Post headline is a geopolitical meta-analysis of the Iran war broadly, not nuclear-specific — off-angle for this sub-corpus

silences.what_they_covered_instead: Russia is a JCPOA co-signatory with ongoing influence over Iran's nuclear calculus; its perspective is entirely absent from this sub-corpus
Iran's own framing of its nuclear program — sovereignty, civilian use, negotiating conditions — is absent; Iran appears only through opposition and adversarial Western outlets
Iraq is Iran's immediate neighbor most exposed to regional nuclear risk and hosts US forces; its perspective on the nuclear story is absent

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet posing a counterfactual policy question in its headline — frames the entire story as a failure-of-diplomacy post-mortem rather than a breaking-news threat
Only outlets leading with specific satellite-imagery evidence of new fortifications at Natanz — operational-intelligence framing absent from all other titles
Only outlet running a historical framing headline ('post-Cold War uranium program proved old foes can work together') — counter-narrative to the threat-dominant corpus

bottom_line: Iran nuclear headlines are almost entirely a threat-proximity story filtered through US and Israeli framing, with Trump-Xi summit alignment as a thin diplomatic counterweight and the JCPOA counterfactual confined to a single opinion outlet.
